ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wafer food product class; no specific cream-filled wafer cube entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000N covers biscuits and similar baked products; no dedicated wafer cube sub-code exists. Closest parent category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cream-filled wafer cube snack product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of wafer cubes is approximately 30 g (about 6–8 small cubes), providing roughly 157 kcal, 8.1 g fat, and 19 g carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Wafer cube (any flavour) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Wafer cube (any flavour)', CR ID F11080077. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Wafers, vanilla — Glycemic Index Database (analogous estimate: plain cream wafer biscuit)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for 'wafer cube (any flavour)'. The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 76 for plain cream-filled wafer biscuits (vanilla wafer type). This is used as the closest defensible analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (76 × 19.0 g) / 100 = 14.4. Available carbohydrate per 30 g serving = 63.44 × 0.30 = 19.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (30.4 g/100 g) promoting insulin-driven sodium retention; sodium at 72 mg/100 g is moderate but cumulative intake risk exists with habitual snacking
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit wafer cube consumption due to high sugar content (30.4 g/100 g), which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and may elevate blood pressure independently of dietary sodium; a single 30 g serving delivers approximately 9 g of sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~76), high sugar (30.4 g/100 g), and high refined carbohydrate (63.4 g/100 g) with only 2.1 g/100 g dietary fibre, resulting in rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Wafer cubes are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to a high glycemic index (~76), high sugar content (30.4 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g), which together produce a significant glycemic load of approximately 14.4 per 30 g serving; avoidance or strict portion control is strongly recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (27 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~13.5 g/100 g from palm-based vegetable fat, combined with high refined sugar (30.4 g/100 g), elevating LDL cholesterol and triglyceride risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid wafer cubes due to high saturated fat content (estimated ~13.5 g/100 g from palm oil-based filling) and high refined sugar (30.4 g/100 g), both of which independently raise LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels, increasing atherosclerotic risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wafer cubes are small, cubic-shaped baked snack biscuits consisting of thin, crisp wafer layers sandwiched with a sweet cream filling, available in a variety of flavours including vanilla, chocolate, strawberry, and pandan. They are an ultra-processed snack product manufactured widely across Asia and internationally, typically using wheat flour, vegetable fat (commonly palm oil), sugar, flavouring agents, and emulsifiers. The texture is light and crunchy with a sweet, creamy interior. Per 100 g, wafer cubes provide 522 kcal, 5.5 g protein, 27 g fat (predominantly saturated from palm oil), and 63.4 g carbohydrates of which 30.4 g is sugar, with only 2.1 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 76 (high), making them a high-glycemic snack with a glycemic load of ~14.4 per 30 g serving. Clinically, wafer cubes are unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to their high refined sugar, high saturated fat, and low fibre profile. They are commonly consumed as a casual snack in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, often sold in small individual packs. Sodium content is relatively moderate at 72 mg/100 g. Allergens include gluten and wheat; may contain milk and soy depending on manufacturer.
common_user_queries: How many calories in wafer cubes?
Is wafer cube high in sugar?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11080077) at confidence 0.95. The food entry is an exact match. Saturated fat is estimated at ~13.5 g/100 g based on typical palm oil-based wafer product composition as no direct saturated fat figure was provided in the HPB record; this field is therefore an informed estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cream-filled wafer biscuits.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product. Detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this generic wafer cube record as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g is estimated at 13.5 g/100 g based on typical palm oil-based cream wafer product composition; this is not directly reported by HPB and should be verified against product label data. GI value of 76 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cream-filled vanilla wafer biscuits; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. This food is classified as ultra-processed (NOVA Group 4). Halal status is not confirmed and depends on manufacturer certification. The input health_suitability entries for 'diabetes', 'hypertension', and 'rheumatoid_arthritis' have been reviewed and reformulated into the standardised health_context structure with corrected condition keys (type2_diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular_disease). Rheumatoid arthritis has been replaced by cardiovascular_disease as a mandatory condition per Veritas scoring requirements; rheumatoid arthritis clinical notes are preserved in the guidance rationale where relevant.
